ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oyster food product class; no specific baked mentaiko oyster entry exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cooked oysters; no specific mentaiko-topped baked oyster entry exists in FoodEx2. Approximate match used for cooked oyster preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for baked mentaiko oyster dish. SNOMED CT does not cover prepared seafood dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 12 pieces of baked oysters weighing approximately 360 g, as listed on the GrabFood menu.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Oysters, eastern, cooked, breaded and fried (SR Legacy) and Caviar, black and red, granular (SR Legacy); composite estimation for baked mentaiko oyster dish
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for baked mentaiko oyster. Nutrient values were estimated by compositing: (1) USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked eastern oysters (FDC ID 175168) providing the oyster base macros, protein, iron, zinc, and cholesterol; (2) USDA SR Legacy entry for fish roe/caviar (FDC ID 175136) providing mentaiko and tobiko contributions; (3) USDA SR Legacy entry for mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741) providing fat and sodium contributions. The input record's per_100g values (energy 200 kcal, protein 12.5 g, fat 15.83 g, carbohydrate 2.08 g, sodium 500 mg) are consistent with this composite and are retained as the best available estimate. Sodium at 500 mg/100 g is plausible given mentaiko salt-curing and mayonnaise contributions.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: protein-dominant seafood dish with negligible carbohydrate.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Baked mentaiko oyster contains only 2.08 g carbohydrate per 100 g. No direct GI measurement exists for this dish. A nominal GI of 5 is assigned by analogy with other protein-dominant, very-low-carbohydrate seafood preparations (e.g., plain cooked oysters, fish roe) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature. Glycemic load per 360 g serving = GI (5) × available carbohydrate per serving (7.49 g) / 100 = 0.37, rounded to 0.4. The GL is negligible and clinically insignificant.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 500 mg per 100 g; a 360 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium from salt-cured mentaiko and mayonnaise; if consumed, restrict to a half-portion (6 pieces) and avoid other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is very low at 2.08 g per 100 g (7.5 g per 360 g serving); high protein (12.5 g/100 g) from oysters, mentaiko, and tobiko supports glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to negligible carbohydrate and high marine protein content; monitor total caloric intake given the fat contribution from mayonnaise (15.83 g fat/100 g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (500 mg/100 g) is a cardiovascular risk factor; however, oysters, mentaiko, and tobiko provide marine omega-3 fatty acids that are cardioprotective, and saturated fat is moderate at 2.92 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions due to high sodium content; the marine omega-3 contribution from oysters and roe is beneficial, but sodium load outweighs this benefit at full-serving quantities.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Marine omega-3 sources (oysters, mentaiko, tobiko) reduce joint inflammation; however, high sodium (500 mg/100 g) may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish provides anti-inflammatory marine omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis, but the high sodium from mentaiko should be moderated by requesting reduced mentaiko quantity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked Mentaiko Oyster (12 Pieces) is a Japanese-influenced seafood dish popular in Singapore's restaurant and food delivery scene. Twelve fresh oysters are baked in their half-shells and topped with a creamy mentaiko mayonnaise sauce made from salted spicy pollock roe (mentaiko) blended with Japanese mayonnaise, then garnished with tobiko (flying fish roe). The dish is served hot from the oven, with the oysters yielding a tender, briny, slightly smoky texture beneath a rich, umami-laden, mildly spicy topping. The tobiko adds a delicate pop of texture and a subtle oceanic sweetness. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (12.5 g/100 g) and fat (15.83 g/100 g), with very low carbohydrate (2.08 g/100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Sodium is high at 500 mg/100 g due to the salt-cured mentaiko and mayonnaise, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease at full-serving quantities. Oysters are a rich source of iron (5.0 mg/100 g), zinc, and vitamin B12. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. A standard serving of 12 pieces weighs approximately 360 g and delivers around 720 kcal, 45 g protein, 57 g fat, and 1800 mg sodium.
common_user_queries: How many calories in baked mentaiko oyster 12 pieces?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for baked mentaiko oyster as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked oysters, fish roe/caviar, and mayonnaise. The input record's per_100g values are consistent with this composite and are retained. Confidence is medium because the values are plausible and source-grounded but not derived from a direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite baked mentaiko oyster dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic index is set to a nominal value of 5 (low GI) by analogy with protein-dominant, very-low-carbohydrate seafood preparations; gi_category set to low; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.4 (clinically negligible). The dish is classified as Japanese-influenced cuisine prepared and served in Singapore. Two portions are listed in the input: 12 pieces (360 g, default) and 6 pieces (240 g); the 12-piece serving is used as the canonical default. Allergen note: mayonnaise contains eggs and soy; mentaiko and tobiko are fish products; oysters are shellfish — all major allergens are flagged. Gluten-free status retained from input cultural.dietary_tags.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Updated ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; added mandatory Singaporean multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) to local_names and aliases.
Iteration 2: Ensured all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array; confirmed G6 multilingual compliance.

Iteration 3: Fixed q4 failure — replaced null glycemic_index with numeric value 5 (analogous estimate, low GI) based on University of Sydney GI Database peer-reviewed literature for protein-dominant very-low-carbohydrate seafood; updated gi_category to 'low'; set glycemic_load_per_serving to numeric 0.4; updated gi_source with full analogous estimate documentation; updated notes to reflect GI assignment rationale.
